subject: english
learning_objectives: Define a noun and give examples across different categories
Identify the main types of nouns and explain their characteristics
Form regular, irregular, compound, and zero plurals correctly

sections.heading: Definition
sections.content:
A noun is a naming word. It names a person, animal, place, thing, or idea. Nouns also include names of institutions, months, days, and abstract ideas.

Examples by category:
‚Ä¢ People: Uche, Peterson, Adebisi, Falase
‚Ä¢ Places: Beijing, Atan Ota, London, Sweden, Canada
‚Ä¢ Things: table, chair, house, laptop, radio
‚Ä¢ Institutions: family, tribe, Christianity, Islam, university
‚Ä¢ Months/Days: January, February, Sunday, Thursday
‚Ä¢ Abstract ideas: beauty, knowledge, emotion, hope, courage, wisdom, empathy

sections.heading: Features of Nouns
sections.content:
1. Most nouns form their plurals by adding -s or -es: girl‚Üígirls, box‚Üíboxes, church‚Üíchurches.
2. Nouns are often used with articles, demonstratives, and adjectives: a cup, that house, some people.
3. Words ending with the following morphemes are usually nouns:
   -age (damage, homage, stoppage)
   -al (arrival, dismissal, refusal)
   -tion (action, association, imagination)
   -er (adviser, teacher, worker)
   -ery (machinery, slavery, stationery)
   -titude (servitude, solitude)
   -hood (boyhood, girlhood, childhood)
   -ist (bicyclist, motorist, socialist)
   -ity (ability, brevity, equity)
   -ment (arrangement, establishment, government)
   -ness (firmness, fairness, laziness)
   -cy (proficiency, ascendancy, legacy)
   -ocracy (democracy, aristocracy, autocracy)
   -ism (Zionism, Feudalism, Communism)
   -ship (scholarship, fellowship, membership)
   -ster (gangster, trickster, youngster)

sections.heading: Types of Nouns
sections.content:
Proper Nouns ‚Äì Name a SPECIFIC person, place, or thing. Always begin with a capital letter.
  Examples: Atan-Ota, Monday, August, Ade, Ottawa, January

Common Nouns ‚Äì Name things/people/places of a general kind.
  Examples: boy, man, lady, church, mosque, table

Concrete Nouns ‚Äì Can be seen and touched.
  Examples: books, tables, bag

Abstract Nouns ‚Äì Exist only as concepts; cannot be seen or touched, only felt.
  Examples: hatred, hunger, pain, intelligence

Count Nouns ‚Äì Can be counted; have singular and plural forms.
  Examples: one man‚Üífive men, one orange‚Üíseveral oranges, a book‚Üífive books

Non-count / Mass Nouns ‚Äì Cannot be counted; have only singular form.
  Examples: sand, soap, rice, homework, water
  (May be counted through units: three bags of rice, a bar of soap)

Other uncountable nouns: equipment, jewelry, stationery, information, baggage, luggage, machinery, furniture, damage.
  NOTE: These must never take -s/-es to form plurals.
  Correct: 'All students were instructed to take their baggage.' (NOT baggages)
  Correct: 'We have got information about them.' (NOT informations)
  NOTE: 'damages' = a legal fine; 'damage' = physical harm.

Collective Nouns ‚Äì Name a group of people or things.
  A troupe of dancers | A troop of soldiers | A band of thieves | A bevy of ladies | A class of students

Possessive/Genitive Nouns ‚Äì Indicate possession.
  Examples: Dr Oyeyemi's car, Mrs Alalade's dress, Mr Jayeola's house

sections.heading: Number: Singular and Plural
sections.content:
Regular Plurals (-s / -es):
  school‚Üíschools, mat‚Üímats, house‚Üíhouses, box‚Üíboxes, bonus‚Üíbonuses

Irregular Plurals:
  man‚Üímen, ox‚Üíoxen, goose‚Üígeese, crisis‚Üícrises, forum‚Üífora,
  formula‚Üíformulae, symposium‚Üísymposia, foot‚Üífeet,
  parenthesis‚Üíparentheses, medium‚Üímedia, index‚Üíindices/indexes,
  larva‚Üílarvae, louse‚Üílice, mouse‚Üímice, curriculum‚Üícurricula,
  axis‚Üíaxes, oasis‚Üíoases

Plurals in Compound Nouns (the principal noun takes the plural):
  head of state‚Üíheads of state, commander in chief‚Üícommanders in chief,
  woman doctor‚Üíwomen doctors, secretary general‚Üísecretaries general,
  passer-by‚Üípassers-by, mother-in-law‚Üímothers-in-law, grown-up‚Üígrown-ups

Zero Plurals (same form singular and plural):
  gross, deer, sheep, fish/fishes, series, trout, salmon, person/persons/people

Always plural in form:
  aircraft, cattle

Always take -s (naturally plural in meaning):
  goods, remains, ashes, headquarters, quarters, congratulations, manners,
  surroundings, wages, arms, works (public works)
